figures.caption: Figure 1. Representative samples from CropAndWeed [33] and leaf-level annotations for our CropAndWeedAndLeaf benchmark, along with results of the multi-domain ReLeaf model, illustrating its generalization to unseen species.

Figure 4. Comparison of leaf-segmentation performance (% mAP50–95) and inference time (ms) for multiple model-architecture variants and input resolutions on the PhenoBench test set. Selected variants providing a reasonable trade-off for real-time deployment are marked by circles.

Table 3. Overview of 23 plant species included in CropAndWeedAndLeaf with leaf-segmentation annotations for 15 samples each, along with corresponding instance-segmentation performance (% mAP50-95). Scores are results of the selected model architecture (YOLO26 Medium, input size of 7682 pixels), trained on the combined ReLeaf dataset.

Figure 2. Representative processing pipeline, illustrating the typical context of leaf segmentation. Image patches depicting individual detected plants are cropped from larger-resolution field recordings for fine-grained analysis. While the upstream tasks of plant-level detection and instance segmentation are well researched for common species, tasks related to leaf-segmentation remain underrepresented.

Figure 9. Representative leaf-segmentation results of YOLO26 models (Medium, 7682 pixels input resolution) trained on LSC [31], KOmatsuna [35], GrowliFlower [18] and PhenoBench [41] datasets and their combination (ReLeaf ), evaluated on all 23 species of the CropAndWeedAndLeaf benchmark, along with corresponding ground truth (GT) (Part 1).

Figure 10. Representative leaf-segmentation results of YOLO26 models (Medium, 7682 pixels input resolution) trained on LSC [31], KOmatsuna [35], GrowliFlower [18] and PhenoBench [41] datasets and their combination (ReLeaf ), evaluated on all 23 species of the CropAndWeedAndLeaf benchmark, along with corresponding ground truth (GT) (Part 2).

Figure 3. Representative images from leaf-segmentation datasets, depicting Realistic field settings [18, 41] and controlled Laboratory environments [31, 35], or Excluded due to incompatible leaf annotations [2, 34, 36].

Figure 8. Representative leaf-segmentation results of selected YOLO26 configuration (Medium with an input resolution of 7682 pixels) trained on a combination of LSC [31], Komatsuna (KO) [35], GrowliFlower (GF) [18] and PhenoBench (PB) [41] datasets and evaluated on the respective test sets.

Figure 6. Representative leaf-segmentation results of YOLO26 models (Medium, 7682 pixels input resolution) trained on LSC [31], KOmatsuna [35], GrowliFlower [18] and PhenoBench [41] datasets and their combination (ReLeaf ) on the CropAndWeedAndLeaf benchmark, along with corresponding ground truth (GT).

Table 1. Key statistics of selected, excluded and newly annotated datasets: Annotations of leaves (either per Plant, per Image or as classification labels for leaf Counting), numbers of Plants in the original dataset and Selected patches with a a minimum area of 600 pixels (unknown for Airphen due to image-level leaf annotations), numbers of plant Species, median original Image sizes, median cropped Patch sizes of selected instances, recording Sensors and Settings.

Table 5. Comparison of bounding-box (Box) and segmentationmask (Mask) accuracies (% mAP50–95) and inference times (t) (ms) of leaf-segmentation for multiple model-architecture variants and input resolutions on the PhenoBench test set. Selected variants providing a reasonable trade-off for real-time deployment are highlighted by bold text.

Figure 7. Comparison of bounding-box accuracy (% mAP50–95) and inference time (ms) of leaf-segmentation for multiple modelarchitecture variants and input resolutions on the PhenoBench test set. Selected variants providing a reasonable trade-off for realtime deployment are marked by circles.
